primary_hypothesis: The West vendor staffing transition caused poorer handling performance, producing a higher incident propensity than East among items of the same severity during the observation window.
rival_hypothesis: The aggregate regional disparity is a severity-composition effect: West received a much larger share of high-severity items, while West and East had no material incident-rate difference within the recorded severity strata.
predicted_discrimination: A material within-severity West/East gap, especially one consistently unfavorable to West, would support the staffing-related performance candidate over the severity-composition rival. A zero or negligible gap in both severity strata would reject that candidate as an explanation of the recorded regional gap and support the composition rival; mixed or appreciable stratum-specific gaps would leave both explanations partly viable and require further study.
bounded_conclusion: For the supplied observation window and the two recorded severity strata, the records support unequal severity composition as the explanation for the West/East aggregate incident-rate disparity; they do not support attributing the disparity to worse same-severity handling following the West staffing transition.
residual_uncertainty: The records do not establish whether severity assignments were comparable across regions or whether unmeasured factors within the high- and low-severity strata differed; they also provide no pre-transition or item-level evidence capable of testing other effects of the staffing change.
recommended_consequence: Do not blame or sanction the vendor on this record. Escalate for a reversible review of routing and severity-classification comparability and collect item-level, pre/post-transition evidence before making any causal or vendor-action decision; no sanction authority is supplied here.
